Agreement on the extremes

All three formulae agree without qualification on the most and least consonant
intervals. The unison (1), octave (2), fifth (3/2), and fourth (4/3) are
assigned the lowest values by every measure, consistent with their historical
priority in counterpoint and their prominence in non-Western musical systems.
The tritone (45/32) receives the highest value under every formula, and the
minor and major seconds cluster near the top of the dissonance scale in all
three cases. The disagreements are concentrated in the middle of the table,
among intervals that are also the most contested in historical music theory.

Correlation and ties

Euler‚Äôs Gradus achieves a Spearman rank correlation of œÅ=0.979\rho=0.979 against
the human data; ff and max\max both achieve œÅ=0.989\rho=0.989. The difference is
modest, and caution is warranted given the small sample (13 intervals) and the
cultural specificity of the rating data (see Section¬Ý6). More informative than
the correlation coefficient is the tie structure. Gradus produces two tie
groups among the 13 intervals: the major third and major sixth share G=7G=7,
and the minor third, minor sixth, and major second share G=8G=8. The
three-way tie at G=8G=8 is particularly awkward, as the three intervals span
three consecutive human ranks (6, 8, 9). The formula ff resolves this
three-way tie ‚Äî giving f=10f=10, 1212, 1212 respectively ‚Äî though it
introduces a two-way tie between the minor sixth and major second (both f=12f=12, human ranks 8 and 9). The metric max‚Å°(p,q)\max(p,q) shares the major third /
major sixth tie with ff, but adds a further tie between the major second and
minor seventh (both max=9\max=9, human ranks 9 and 10). On the criterion of
tie-resolution among these 13 intervals, ff performs best, Gradus
intermediate, and max\max least well.

Dynamic range and the tritone

The three formulae differ most sharply in their treatment of the tritone. Gradus
gives G‚Äã(45/32)=14G(45/32)=14, only four units above the major seventh (G=10G=10).
Both ff and max\max assign substantially higher values (f=50f=50, max=45\max=45),
placing the tritone in a category of its own and reflecting the large
numerator p=45p=45 that the asymmetric formulae weight directly. Whether this
wider dynamic range better represents human experience is difficult to assess
from rank-correlation data alone; what can be said is that the tritone is
consistently rated as by far the most dissonant interval in the human data,
and both ff and max\max capture that separation more emphatically than Gradus.

A caveat is necessary: the tritone used throughout this paper is 45/3245/32, the
5-limit augmented fourth generated within the diatonic scale as
45/32=(9/8)‚ãÖ(5/4)45/32=(9/8)\cdot(5/4). This choice is conventional in 5-limit just
intonation but is not the only defensible one. The septimal tritone 7/57/5
(582.5 cents) gives f‚Äã(7/5)=7+Œ©‚àó‚Äã(5)=11f(7/5)=7+\Omega^{\!*}(5)=11 and G‚Äã(7/5)=11G(7/5)=11 ‚Äî barely
more dissonant than the major third under either measure. The undecimal tritone
11/811/8 gives f=14f=14. These alternatives sit in different prime limits and
carry different musical connotations: 7/57/5 is the tritone of barbershop and
some jazz practice; 45/3245/32 is the tritone of classical diatonic harmony.
The extreme values f=50f=50 and max=45\max=45 are therefore properties of the
5-limit diatonic tritone specifically, not of the tritone concept in general,
and should be interpreted accordingly. The high values are, however,
consistent with the 5-limit diatonic context in which the human rating data
were collected.

Symmetry

Gradus is symmetric in pp and qq: G‚Äã(p/q)=G‚Äã(q/p)G(p/q)=G(q/p). This reflects Euler‚Äôs
focus on the product p‚ãÖqp\cdot q as the relevant arithmetic quantity, and is
appropriate if the perceptual roles of the two notes are interchangeable.
Both ff and max\max are asymmetric, treating the numerator (upper note) and
denominator (lower note) differently, as is the case when one note functions
as a bass and the other as a melody note. The symmetry of Gradus means it
cannot distinguish 9/59/5 from 16/916/9 ‚Äî two minor sevenths that differ by
only 1 cent but arise from different tuning systems ‚Äî whereas ff gives
f‚Äã(9/5)=13f(9/5)=13 and f‚Äã(16/9)=20f(16/9)=20. Whether this asymmetry reflects a genuine
perceptual distinction or an artefact of the formula is an open question;
the perceptual hypothesis of Section¬Ý7 offers one possible motivation.

Interval inversion

Two intervals whose frequency ratios multiply to 2 are inversions of
each other within the octave: the major third (5/4) inverts to the minor
sixth (8/5), the fifth (3/2) to the fourth (4/3), and so on. Under Gradus
and under Tenney height, inversionally related intervals receive similar but
not identical values (G‚Äã(5/4)=7G(5/4)=7 vs G‚Äã(8/5)=8G(8/5)=8; H‚Äã(5/4)=20H(5/4)=20 vs
H‚Äã(8/5)=40H(8/5)=40). The formula ff is more strongly asymmetric: f‚Äã(5/4)=7f(5/4)=7
but f‚Äã(8/5)=12f(8/5)=12, a gap of 5 units. This captures the perceptual asymmetry
‚Äî a minor sixth in root position (bass on the lower note) is generally
heard as less stable than a major third ‚Äî but amplifies it considerably.
Whether the magnitude of the asymmetry is empirically justified would require
testing beyond the Krumhansl dataset, which does not distinguish root-position
from inverted voicings.

The metric max‚Å°(p,q)\max(p,q) is strikingly simple, requires no prime factorisation,

The three formulae are more complementary than competing. For a context

6 Limitations: the Major Third and Major Sixth Tie

No simple formula in this class distinguishes the major third (5/45/4, human

Œ©‚àó‚Äã(q)=2\Omega^{\!*}(q)=2 (since Œ©‚àó‚Äã(4)=Œ©‚àó‚Äã(22)=2\Omega^{\!*}(4)=\Omega^{\!*}(2^{2})=2 and Œ©‚àó‚Äã(3)=2\Omega^{\!*}(3)=2). The
distinction requires knowing that q=4=22q=4=2^{2} is simpler than q=3q=3
under octave equivalence, yet the prime-weighting function cannot see this.

The Tenney height H‚Äã(p/q)=p‚ãÖqH(p/q)=p\cdot q [10] ‚Äî the standard
complexity measure in just intonation theory ‚Äî makes the situation worse:
H‚Äã(5/4)=20>H‚Äã(5/3)=15H(5/4)=20>H(5/3)=15, so Tenney ranks the major sixth as
more consonant than the major third, the opposite of human judgments.
Gradus, ff, and max\max all tie the pair; Tenney inverts them.
This failure of Tenney height is a direct consequence of its symmetric and
multiplicative structure: H‚Äã(p/q)=p‚ãÖqH(p/q)=p\cdot q is dominated by whichever of
pp and qq is larger, which for 5/35/3 is q=3q=3 (small) rather than for
5/45/4 where q=4q=4 (larger). The asymmetric formula ff ties them for the
same structural reason ‚Äî Œ©‚àó‚Äã(3)=Œ©‚àó‚Äã(4)=2\Omega^{\!*}(3)=\Omega^{\!*}(4)=2 ‚Äî but does not
invert the order.

Lahdelma & Eerola [2] decompose consonance ratings into
sensory and familiarity components; on sensory consonance alone,
5/45/4 and 5/35/3 are nearly indistinguishable across listeners, suggesting
that the tie in f‚Äã(p/q)f(p/q) may accurately reflect the purely arithmetic
component of the percept. A subsequent study [3] further
shows that register and inversion affect consonance ratings independently of
roughness and harmonicity, providing direct empirical support for the
asymmetric role of the bass note assumed in Section¬Ý7.
The gap observed in Krumhansl‚Äôs data is plausibly
attributable to learned exposure ‚Äî major thirds occur far more frequently
than major sixths in tonal music ‚Äî consistent with the finding of McDermott et al. [4] that
consonance preferences in a culture with minimal Western music exposure are
substantially reduced. The converse view ‚Äî that consonance preferences
reflect universal statistics of harmonic sounds in the environment, including
voiced speech ‚Äî is advanced by Bowling, Purves & Gill [5],
who find that vocal similarity predicts interval attraction across cultures;
on this account the residual consonance of ET thirds may reflect their
partial alignment with vocal harmonic spectra.
Resolving the tie within an arithmetic formula would therefore require
modelling cultural familiarity, which is outside the scope of the present
approach.

7 A Two-Stage Perceptual Hypothesis

We propose a speculative two-stage perceptual interpretation of the formula,
as a qualitative hypothesis about how the auditory system may evaluate an
interval. This is a perceptual hypothesis, not a mathematical conjecture:
it cannot be proved from the formula, but it can be tested experimentally.

Stage 1 ‚Äî Bass establishes harmonic context (cost Œ©‚àó‚Äã(q)\Omega^{\!*}(q)).
The brain does not literally factorise integers: the auditory system performs
subharmonic template matching or autocorrelation across candidate periods
[12, 13]. The prime decomposition Œ©‚àó‚Äã(q)\Omega^{\!*}(q) acts as an
analytical proxy for this biological process, counting the
octave-reduction and prime-ascent steps needed to reach the implied
fundamental, and mirroring the complexity of the template search without
claiming that the brain performs explicit prime factorisation.

In this model, the lower note is the qq-th partial of an implied fundamental
FF. The brain extrapolates downward to FF by ‚Äúdividing out‚Äù the prime
factors of¬Ýqq: each factor of 2 (an octave) costs 1 unit; each factor of 3
costs¬Ý2; each factor of 5 costs¬Ý4. This is exactly Œ©‚àó‚Äã(q)\Omega^{\!*}(q). Intervals whose
bass note is a power of 2 (e.g. the octave, q=1q=1; the major second bass,
q=8=23q=8=2^{3}) establish the fundamental cheaply via octave equivalence. Intervals
with an odd prime in qq (the fourth, q=3q=3; the minor third bass, q=5q=5)
require the brain to extrapolate through a genuinely new prime, incurring
higher cost. The subharmonic matching mechanism is formalised by Parncutt
[12], extending Terhardt‚Äôs virtual pitch theory to chord roots.

Stage 2 ‚Äî Upper note reaches into the series (cost pp).
Once the fundamental is established, the upper note must be recognised as its
pp-th partial. Higher partials are quieter (amplitude ‚àù1/p\propto 1/p for
harmonic sounds with a falling spectral envelope, such as bowed strings and
the singing voice; the exact roll-off is instrument-dependent), more narrowly
tuned, more sensitive to mistuning, and have lower pitch salience
[13]. The cost of confirming the upper note as a member of the
harmonic series is therefore modelled as pp itself.

Hypothesised total perceptual effort: f‚Äã(p/q)=p+Œ©‚àó‚Äã(q)f(p/q)=p+\Omega^{\!*}(q).

This hypothesis is consistent with:

‚Ä¢

Galileo (1638): pulse coincidences as physical mechanism;

‚Ä¢

Rameau (1722): the corps sonore (harmonic series) as
the source of musical meaning;

‚Ä¢

Euler (1739): prime arithmetic as the language of harmonic
complexity;

‚Ä¢

Terhardt (1979): virtual pitch and harmonic template matching
as auditory mechanisms;

‚Ä¢

Huron [14]: statistical learning of harmonic patterns as
the basis of consonance judgments.

We note that the formula p+Œ©‚àó‚Äã(q)p+\Omega^{\!*}(q) is not the unique arithmetic expression
consistent with this interpretation: other choices such as p+log‚Å°qp+\log q or
p+Œ©‚Äã(q)p+\Omega(q) (unweighted prime count) are structurally plausible. The
particular fit of Œ©‚àó‚Äã(q)\Omega^{\!*}(q) ‚Äî Euler‚Äôs prime-weighted function ‚Äî to human
data is an empirical observation rather than a theoretical necessity, and
invites further experimental investigation.

Equal temperament

Twelve-tone equal temperament (12-TET) divides the octave into 12 equal
semitones of 100 cents each; no interval except the unison and octave is
exactly just. The pattern is clear: the fourth and fifth have ET errors of
only 2 cents and low formula values; the thirds and sixths have errors of
14‚Äì16 cents and moderate values; the minor second and tritone have both
large errors and the highest complexity values. Each ET semitone relies on
the ear pulling a
mistuned pitch into the low-complexity well of its just target ‚Äî the same
well structure examined in Section¬Ý5 (Comparison of the Three Formulae).

Alternative equal temperaments

The tolerance model makes a natural prediction about which equal divisions of
the octave (EDOs) are acoustically viable for which intervals. An nn-EDO
divides the octave into nn equal steps of 1200/n1200/n cents; an interval is
well-approximated when the nearest step falls within
Œîtol\Delta_{\mathrm{tol}} of the just target. The fifth (tolerance ‚àº10\sim 10¬¢
at A3, œÑ=4\tau=4 Hz) is well-approximated by any EDO whose fifth is within
10 cents of 702 cents: this includes 12-TET (2¬¢ error), 19-TET (7¬¢), 31-TET
(5¬¢), and 53-TET (0.07¬¢), but excludes 5-TET (720¬¢, error 18¬¢) and
7-TET (686¬¢, error 16¬¢). The major third (tolerance ‚àº6\sim 6¬¢ at A3) is
well-approximated only by 19-TET (1.5¬¢ error), 31-TET (0.8¬¢), and 53-TET
(1.4¬¢); 12-TET (14¬¢) fails this test at most registers. This arithmetically
explains the longstanding preference of renaissance-era theorists and
contemporary microtonal composers for 19-TET and 31-TET: these systems
preserve 5-limit harmony within the acoustic tolerance budget, whereas 12-TET
can only do so for the fifth and fourth. The framework thus connects the
arithmetic complexity measure f‚Äã(p/q)f(p/q) to a principled criterion for
evaluating tuning systems.

The minor seventh and competing tunings

The minor seventh provides a striking special case. The Ptolemaic minor
seventh 9/59/5 (996 cents) and the Pythagorean minor seventh 16/916/9 (995
cents) are only 1 cent apart, yet have distinct arithmetic characters.
Gradus gives both the same value (G=9G=9), and is therefore unable to
distinguish these historically competing tunings. The formula ff does
distinguish them: f‚Äã(9/5)=13f(9/5)=13 vs f‚Äã(16/9)=20f(16/9)=20, favouring the Ptolemaic
tuning. The Pythagorean version 16/9=(4/3)216/9=(4/3)^{2} arises as two stacked fourths and

The 1/p1/p duality

The model suggests a duality between the two uses of pp in this framework.
In the dissonance formula f‚Äã(p/q)=p+Œ©‚àó‚Äã(q)f(p/q)=p+\Omega^{\!*}(q), the term pp is the
cost of the interval: the effort required to identify the upper note
as the pp-th partial of an implied fundamental. In the tolerance model, pp
appears in the denominator of Œîtol\Delta_{\mathrm{tol}}: it is the
precision required to keep the partial beating within an acceptable
rate. These two roles of pp are consistent with the same physical fact ‚Äî
that pp is the partial number of the upper note in the harmonic series, and
that both the cognitive effort of recognition and the acoustic sensitivity
to mistuning may scale with partial height.

The implication for equal temperament is that Western musical culture has, over
several centuries, adopted a tuning system in which the acoustically robust
intervals (fifth and fourth, small pp) are preserved almost exactly, while
the acoustically fragile intervals (thirds and sixths, moderate pp) are
mistuned to the edge of ‚Äî and in many registers slightly beyond ‚Äî their
acoustic tolerance. Whether this represents a deliberate optimisation,
cultural habituation, or a combination of the two is a question that the
arithmetic model alone cannot resolve.

Concluding remarks

The main contributions of this note are: (i)¬Ýa reinterpretation of Euler‚Äôs
Gradus Suavitatis as a weighted harmonic coincidence count, under a
discrete model of integer-indexed harmonics, connecting it to Galileo‚Äôs
pulse-coincidence model; (ii)¬Ýthe asymmetric formula f‚Äã(p/q)=p+Œ©‚àó‚Äã(q)f(p/q)=p+\Omega^{\!*}(q),
which matches the best known rank correlation with human consonance data while
resolving more ties than Gradus or max‚Å°(p,q)\max(p,q); (iii)¬Ýa speculative two-stage
perceptual hypothesis offering a cognitive reading of ff; (iv)¬Ýa
partial-beating tolerance model that links the term pp in ff to acoustic
sensitivity to mistuning, with implications for equal temperament; and
(v)¬Ýthe coprime integer triangle T‚Äã(n,k)T(n,k) and its superparticular diagonal,
catalogued as OEIS A397104 and A397106 respectively.

Several directions suggest themselves for future work. The consonance ratings
of Krumhansl [1] are based on a small sample of trained
Western listeners; testing the formula against larger and more culturally
diverse datasets [4, 5] would establish how far
the arithmetic structure of ff generalises beyond that context. The
perceptual hypothesis of Section¬Ý7 calls for direct experimental investigation:
if Stage¬Ý1 cost (bass complexity) and Stage¬Ý2 cost (upper-note partial height)
are genuinely separable, they should be dissociable by manipulating register,
inversion, and timbre independently. The tolerance model of Section¬Ý9 makes a
concrete and testable prediction ‚Äî that mistuning sensitivity scales as
1/p1/p at the first coincident partial ‚Äî which could be evaluated with
psychoacoustic paradigms. Finally, the arithmetic structure of the triangle
T‚Äã(n,k)T(n,k) invites number-theoretic investigation: properties of the row sums,
maxima, and minima remain largely unexplored.

Python

from math import gcd
from sympy import factorint  # pip install sympy; or replace with trial division

def omega_star(n):
"""Omega*(n) = sum of e*(p-1) over prime-power factors p^e || n."""
if n <= 1: return 0
return sum(e * (p - 1) for p, e in factorint(n).items())

def gradus(p, q):
"""Euler‚Äôs Gradus Suavitatis G(p/q)."""
g = gcd(p, q); p, q = p // g, q // g
return 1 + omega_star(p) + omega_star(q)

def f(p, q):
"""Asymmetric formula f(p/q) = p + Omega*(q)."""
g = gcd(p, q); p, q = p // g, q // g
return p + omega_star(q)

def maxpq(p, q):
"""Galileo / Farey metric max(p,q)."""
g = gcd(p, q); p, q = p // g, q // g
return max(p, q)

# OEIS triangle T(n,k): first nine rows
for n in range(1, 10):
row = [f(n, k) for k in range(1, n + 1) if gcd(n, k) == 1]
print(row)

# A397106: superparticular diagonal a(m) = m + A275314(m), m >= 1
# (m is the lower partial of the interval (m+1)/m)
def a(m):
return m + 1 + omega_star(m)

print([a(m) for m in range(1, 46)])
